metadata.title: Entrainment of rhythmic tonal sequences on neural oscillations and the impact on subjective emotion
metadata.note: Original EEG study examining neural entrainment to rhythmic tonal sequences (1-12 Hz presenting rates) and their impact on emotional dimensions (valence, arousal, dominance) in 37 participants.

claims.claim_text: All rhythmic tonal sequences across 12 different presenting rates (1-12 Hz) significantly entrain neural oscillations, with neural responses at target frequencies significantly larger than those at non-target frequencies for both ITPC and EPS measures

statistics.note: Significant interaction between tonal presenting rate and response type (target vs non-target) for both ITPC and EPS. All 12 presenting rates showed significantly larger neural responses at target frequencies (p<0.001 for ITPC, p<0.001 to p=0.004 for EPS)

brain_regions.context: Neural entrainment effects particularly pronounced in frontocentral area, consistent between ITPC and EPS topographies. 64 electrodes in extended 10-20 system.

methodology.task: Listening to 12 tonal sequences with different presenting rates (1-12 Hz), each lasting one minute. Sequences composed of eight musical notes from F major scale.
methodology.control_conditions: Within-subjects design with 12 different presenting rates. Target frequency (matching tonal presenting rate) vs non-target frequencies (neither presenting rate nor harmonics)
methodology.participant_characteristics: 37 participants recruited (3 excluded due to technical issues), 19 males, 18 females, mean age 22.72±1.91 years, normal hearing, normal or corrected-to-normal vision, no history of mental disorders or severe physical illnesses
methodology.analysis_details: EEG recorded from 64 electrodes at 1000 Hz. ITPC (Inter-trial phase coherence) and EPS (Evoked power spectrum) calculated. Epochs of 2s with no overlap. DFT with 0.5 Hz step for 1-15 Hz frequency range.

claims.claim_text: Tonal sequences below 6 Hz decreased participants' valence feelings while increasing their dominance feelings, whereas sequences above 6 Hz increased valence feelings (except 10 Hz) while decreasing dominance feelings

axis_proposal.definition: Change in emotional valence (pleasure-displeasure) after listening to rhythmic tonal sequences, measured on 9-point SAM scale (1-9)
axis_proposal.psychoacoustic_basis: Self-Assessment Manikin (SAM) scale ratings before and after listening, difference calculated (after - before)

statistics.p_value: Dominance interaction: p<0.001, time factor: p=0.057 (marginal). Valence and arousal: no significant effects
statistics.effect_size: Dominance interaction: η²=0.08, time factor: η²=0.10
statistics.note: Dominance dimension significantly affected by interaction between time and frequency. Post hoc: 3 Hz (p=0.032) and 10 Hz (p=0.031) significantly changed dominance. Valence and arousal showed no significant effects at any specific rates.

methodology.task: Listening to 12 tonal sequences with different presenting rates (1-12 Hz), each lasting one minute. Self-reported emotional assessments before and after listening using SAM scales (9-point scales for valence, arousal, dominance).
methodology.control_conditions: Within-subjects design with 12 different presenting rates. Emotional assessments before and after listening to each sequence.
methodology.participant_characteristics: 37 participants (19 males, 18 females, mean age 22.72±1.91 years), normal hearing, normal or corrected-to-normal vision, no history of mental disorders or severe physical illnesses

claims.claim_text: Neural entrainment of tonal sequences with presenting rates above 6 Hz demonstrated a significantly positive correlation between ITPC modulation intensity and absolute changes in emotional valence (r=0.22, p=0.002) and dominance (r=0.16, p=0.030)

axis_proposal.definition: Modulation intensity of neural entrainment measured by Inter-trial Phase Coherence (ITPC), reflecting consistency of phase angles across trials at target frequency
axis_proposal.psychoacoustic_basis: ITPC measures phase alignment across multiple trials at specific frequency, where 1 indicates perfect phase coherence and 0 indicates no coherence

dependent_variable.name: Absolute changes in emotional valence and dominance

axis_proposal.definition: Absolute magnitude of emotional changes (valence and dominance) after listening to rhythmic tonal sequences
axis_proposal.psychoacoustic_basis: Absolute value of difference between post- and pre-task emotional ratings on 9-point SAM scales

statistics.p_value: Valence: r=0.22, p=0.002; Dominance: r=0.16, p=0.030
statistics.note: For sequences above 6 Hz, positive correlation between ITPC modulation intensity and absolute changes in valence and dominance. For sequences below 6 Hz, weak or negligible correlation (valence: r=0.00, p=0.969; dominance: r=-0.05, p=0.549)

brain_regions.context: Neural entrainment particularly pronounced in frontocentral area. ITPC modulation intensity averaged across electrodes correlated with emotional changes.

methodology.task: Listening to 12 tonal sequences with different presenting rates (1-12 Hz), each lasting one minute. ITPC calculated at target frequency, averaged across electrodes. Emotional changes (post- vs pre-task) measured using SAM scales.
methodology.control_conditions: Within-subjects design with 12 different presenting rates. Correlation analysis between entrainment magnitude and emotional changes for sequences above 6 Hz.
methodology.analysis_details: Pearson correlation coefficients computed between entrainment magnitude (averaged across electrodes) and standardized emotion change scores (post- vs pre-task) for sequences above 6 Hz

c3_associations.process: Rhythmic tonal sequences at different presenting rates (1-12 Hz) entrain neural oscillations, with entrainment effects particularly pronounced in frontocentral area, suggesting sensorimotor timing mechanisms

brain_regions.role: Neural entrainment to rhythmic tonal sequences

c3_associations.evidence: All 12 presenting rates (1-12 Hz) significantly entrain neural oscillations, with ITPC and EPS showing clear peaks at target frequencies and harmonics. Entrainment effects particularly pronounced in frontocentral area. Presenting rates correspond to tempo: 1 Hz = 60 bpm, 6 Hz = 360 bpm, 12 Hz = 720 bpm.

c3_associations.process: Rhythmic tonal sequences modulate emotional valence and dominance in frequency-dependent manner, with sequences below 6 Hz decreasing valence and increasing dominance, while sequences above 6 Hz increasing valence and decreasing dominance

brain_regions.role: Neural entrainment correlated with emotional changes

c3_associations.evidence: Tonal sequences below 6 Hz decreased valence and increased dominance. Sequences above 6 Hz increased valence (except 10 Hz) and decreased dominance. 3 Hz and 10 Hz particularly altered dominance levels. For sequences above 6 Hz, ITPC modulation intensity positively correlated with changes in valence (r=0.22, p=0.002) and dominance (r=0.16, p=0.030).

c3_associations.process: Neural entrainment to rhythmic tonal sequences measured by ITPC (phase coherence) and EPS (evoked power spectrum) shows frequency-specific synchronization at target frequencies and harmonics
c3_associations.evidence: ITPC and EPS show clear peaks at target frequencies (1-12 Hz) and their harmonics. Neural responses at target frequencies significantly larger than non-target frequencies for all 12 presenting rates (p<0.001). ITPC: F(11,330)=4.81, p<0.001, η²=0.14. EPS: F(11,330)=14.10, p<0.001, η²=0.32.

primary_axes.note: Tonal presenting rates: 1-12 Hz (corresponding to 60-720 bpm). 6 Hz (360 bpm) appears as boundary: sequences below 6 Hz decrease valence and increase dominance, sequences above 6 Hz increase valence and decrease dominance. 3 Hz and 10 Hz particularly effective for dominance modulation.
Presenting rates converted to bpm: 1 Hz = 60 bpm, 6 Hz = 360 bpm, 12 Hz = 720 bpm. 6 Hz (360 bpm) boundary aligns with theta-alpha transition zone (4-8 Hz theta, 8-10 Hz alpha).

r3_coordinates.note: Tonal presenting rate (L4.rate_hz) is primary independent variable. 6 Hz boundary (360 bpm) separates frequency-dependent emotional effects: below 6 Hz decreases valence and increases dominance, above 6 Hz increases valence and decreases dominance. Neural entrainment intensity (ITPC) correlates with emotional changes for sequences above 6 Hz.

mentions.context: Neural entrainment effects particularly pronounced in frontocentral area, consistent between ITPC and EPS topographies
Neural entrainment particularly pronounced in frontocentral area. ITPC modulation intensity averaged across electrodes correlated with emotional changes
Neural entrainment to rhythmic tonal sequences
Neural entrainment correlated with emotional changes

brain_regions_summary.note: All brain regions identified through EEG electrode placement in extended 10-20 system (64 electrodes). Frontocentral area identified as region with particularly pronounced neural entrainment effects. No specific MNI coordinates or precise Brodmann area confirmation, but frontocentral area typically corresponds to BA6 (premotor/supplementary motor), BA8 (frontal eye fields), and BA9 (dorsolateral prefrontal cortex).

warnings: 3 DV/IV r3_axis unmapped (axis_proposal provided for all)
EEG electrode-based brain region identification (no MNI coordinates or precise Brodmann area confirmation)
Arousal dimension showed no significant effects (turbulent changes)

data_quality.note: Original EEG study with 31 participants (37 recruited, 3 excluded due to technical issues, 3 more excluded for <90% valid trials). All statistics reported (ANOVA F-values, p-values, effect sizes η², correlation coefficients). Brain regions identified through EEG electrode placement. R³ coordinates mapped for presenting rates (L4.rate_hz) and tempo (L6.tempo_bpm). 6 Hz boundary (360 bpm) separates frequency-dependent emotional effects.
